content.claim: Finland's transition from a two-tier (grammar/civic) system to comprehensive schooling (1972–1977) was associated with rising academic performance and lower between-school variance by the 1980s-1990s, but this took approximately two decades to materialize and occurred alongside other simultaneous reforms including teacher master's-degree requirements and curriculum redesign.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: Causal attribution of equity and performance gains to comprehensive structure alone versus concurrent reforms (teacher training, welfare expansion, curriculum) cannot be cleanly separated from available sources
Initial PISA results (2001) validated the model retrospectively, but causality remains debated within Finland
Finnish demographic and institutional context (homogeneity, welfare state capacity, teacher professionalism norms) differs substantially from Hungary
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
